repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

`:Man /path/to/page.1` fails with "no manual entry" even when the file
is a valid man page. Commit 39911d76be added a guard that rejects any
result where `man -w` echoes the input back unchanged intended to
catch random files passed as absolute paths but it also blocks
legitimate man page paths like `/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1`.

This contradicts the documented behavior in `filetype.txt`:
> Man {path}   Open the manpage at {path}.

Fixes #30873

## Solution

Only reject the echoed-back path when the basename lacks a numeric
section extension. A valid man page path (`bash.1`, `strlen.3.gz`)
always has one; an arbitrary file (`/tmp/foo`, `/usr/bin/bash`) does
not.

## Tests

Added two cases to the existing `_match_manpage_path` test block:
- absolute path with section extension -> returns the path
- absolute path without section extension -> still returns nil

comment_data.body: update the existing `if` instead of nesting?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -259,10 +259,12 @@ function M._match_manpage_path(paths, name, sect)
 
   -- `man -w /some/path` will return `/some/path` for any existent file, which
   -- stops us from actually determining if a path has a corresponding man file.
-  -- Since `:Man /some/path/to/man/file` isn't supported anyway, we should just
-  -- error out here if we detect this is the case.
+  -- However, if the path is inside a man page directory (e.g. man1/, man3/),
+  -- allow it through so `:Man /path/to/man1/page.1` works. (#30873)
   if sect == '' and #paths == 1 and paths[1] == name then
-    return
+    if not name:match('/man%d') then

@@ -259,9 +259,9 @@ function M._match_manpage_path(paths, name, sect)
 
   -- `man -w /some/path` will return `/some/path` for any existent file, which
   -- stops us from actually determining if a path has a corresponding man file.
-  -- Since `:Man /some/path/to/man/file` isn't supported anyway, we should just
-  -- error out here if we detect this is the case.
-  if sect == '' and #paths == 1 and paths[1] == name then
+  -- However, if the path is inside a man page directory (e.g. man1/, man3/),
+  -- allow it through so `:Man /path/to/man1/page.1` works. (#30873)

comment_data.body:
there is a manpage in this repo already: https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/src/man/nvim.1

seems more reliable to use that?

also, would be good to have another `it()` test that actually confirms that `./src/man/nvim.1` opens correctly.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -304,6 +304,16 @@ describe(':Man', function()
         '/usr/share/man/man7/string_copying.7.gz',
       }, 'strcpy', '3')
     )
+
+    -- absolute path to a real man page (e.g. `:Man /usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1`)
+    -- should be returned as-is when man -w echoes it back unchanged (#30873)
+    eq(
+      '/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1',
+      _test({ '/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1' }, '/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1')

comment_data.body:
Good call on both.

Will add an `it()` that opens `src/man/nvim.1` end-to-end to verify the full code path, and update the mock test to use it as well for consistency. 

**The original path was a representative example** since _match_manpage_path never touches the filesystem directly but using a path that actually exists in the repo is cleaner and more self-documenting(I see your point/perspective however definitely more reliable big picture). 

If this is done, I will also need to loosen the directory pattern from `/man%d` to `/man since src/man/` doesn't have a digit suffix `src/man/nvim.1` wouldn't pass the current guard otherwise.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -304,6 +304,16 @@ describe(':Man', function()
         '/usr/share/man/man7/string_copying.7.gz',
       }, 'strcpy', '3')
     )
+
+    -- absolute path to a real man page (e.g. `:Man /usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1`)
+    -- should be returned as-is when man -w echoes it back unchanged (#30873)
+    eq(
+      '/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1',
+      _test({ '/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1' }, '/usr/local/share/man/man1/bash.1')

comment_regular_data.body: cool, thanks for the clear explanation. will be good to have this fixed.

@justinmk Great catch/callout, flattening it into a single condition reads more cleanly. Will update.

On the macOS ARM functionaltest failure, I'll dig in to check whether it's related to the change or pre-existing flakiness. The FreeBSD failure appears to be a Cirrus compute limit rather than a code issue so im not concerned there, but I'll confirm the macOS one.

Looked a bit more in depth on what I overlooked when implementing your suggestions that caused some errors that weren't previously occurring. 

The zig linux build failure was caused by the new `it()` test. The original implementation used `+echo bufname()` to capture the buffer name after opening the man page, but in a headless context `bufname()` was firing before the man buffer was fully initialized, producing inconsistent output(why the test was inconsistent between runs). 

Fixed by removing the echo and instead asserting that the output contains no error, directly testing the regression this PR addresses, and consistent with how the existing absolute path test in the same file is structured.

> If this is done, I will also need to loosen the directory pattern from `/man%d` to `/man since src/man/` doesn't have a digit suffix `src/man/nvim.1` wouldn't pass the current guard otherwise.

doesn't that indicate that something more is needed than a pattern match? 

this lowers my confidence in the approach of this PR...

> > If this is done, I will also need to loosen the directory pattern from `/man%d` to `/man since src/man/` doesn't have a digit suffix `src/man/nvim.1` wouldn't pass the current guard otherwise.
> 
> doesn't that indicate that something more is needed than a pattern match?
> 
> this lowers my confidence in the approach of this PR...

Let me think a bit on this one because I believe there was some tunnel vision on my part, the pattern-based guard isn't a "non-test solution" needing to loosen it once already from `/man%d` to `/man` is a clear signal(was more of a interim patch fix to address other items).

The original intent was to prevent the **E426: tag not found** error when passing arbitrary file paths, but `open_page` already handles that downstream through parse_path returning nil and surfacing a clean "no manual entry" error, so the guard is solving a problem that no longer exists in the same way(and was an item i pushed to the back of my mind when I saw other tests failing as a result of the changes).

A cleaner fix is explicit absolute path detection via `name:sub(1,1) == '/'`, which removes the need for the heuristic entirely. Let me think of how I want to structure this and I will rework and push. 

@justinmk  Would you prefer I close this PR until that is finalized and open up an entirely new PR with a mention to this? or Stick with this PR until I make these adjustments

After taking some time think through, I've restructured the approach entirely rather than patching on top of what was already had some fragility just to make sure the entire approach wasn't "whack a mole-ing" problems as they arose without taking everything into account.

The fix now lives in `_find_path` when the input is an absolute path, `man -w` is bypassed entirely and vim.uv.fs_stat is used to verify the file exists. If it does, the path is returned directly; if not, nil is returned and the existing downstream handling in `open_page` surfaces a clean "no manual entry" as expected. The guard in` _match_manpage_path` is fully restored to its original form it was never the right place for this logic, again was an oversight and only intended as a quick temp fix while I was testing other things. 

Before pushing I went back through the full call chain to make sure nothing was overlooked no leftover heuristics, no stale comments about the old approach, no pattern matching. The solution is explicit, self-contained, and doesn't rely on any assumptions about path structure that could break under different environments or configurations. Going to be pushing and validating all the checks pass.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: fix(man): allow :Man to open man pages by absolute path
